```python
def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("You need to schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for half an hour on Monday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"]]}, "Bob": {"Monday": []}}}'
    """

```

    """Find valid meeting slots that work for all participants.

    Args:
        problem_text: The original problem text (same string you passed to
            parse_schedules).

            Pass the raw JSON string as-is — do NOT parse it into a dict first.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a list of slot dicts, sorted by day order
        then start time:
        [{"day": str, "start": str, "end": str}, ...]
        Each slot is at least duration_minutes long. Use 24-hour "HH:MM" format.

        ...     "Schedule Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday 9-17. Alice busy 9:00-10:00.",

```

    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("You need to schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for half an hour on Monday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings.")

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("You need to schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for half an hour on Monday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"]]}, "Bob": {"Monday": []}}}'
    """

```

- Deborah: [9:00-10:00], [10:30-12:00], [12:30-13:00], [13:30-14:00], [14:30-15:30], [16:00-16:30]

- 9:30 = 30

- 14:30 = 330

- 15:30 = 390
- 16:00 = 420
- 16:30 = 450
- 17:00 = 480

Stephanie:

- 14:30-15:00: 330 to 360

Joe:
- 9:00-9:30: 0 to 30
- 10:00-12:00: 60 to 180
- 12:30-13:00: 210 to 240
- 14:00-17:00: 300 to 480

Diana:
- 9:00-10:30: 0 to 90
- 11:30-12:00: 150 to 180
- 13:00-14:00: 240 to 300
- 14:30-15:30: 330 to 390
- 16:00-17:00: 420 to 480

Deborah:
- 9:00-10:00: 0 to 60
- 10:30-12:00: 90 to 180
- 12:30-13:00: 210 to 240
- 13:30-14:00: 270 to 300
- 14:30-15:30: 330 to 390
- 16:00-16:30: 420 to 450

Now, combine all these intervals to get the union of busy times.

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("You need to schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for half an hour on Monday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"]]}, "Bob": {"Monday": []}}}'
    """

```

Looking at the input data, we need to find 30-minute slots on Monday between 9:00 and 17:00 that are free for all four participants: Andrew, Dennis, Nancy, and Alexander.

First, let's list all the busy intervals for each participant on Monday:

- Andrew: No meetings (free all day)
- Dennis:
  - 9:30-10:00
  - 12:00-12:30
  - 14:00-14:30
  - 15:30-16:00
- Nancy:
  - 9:30-11:00
  - 11:30-12:30
  - 14:00-15:00
  - 16:00-16:30
- Alexander:
  - 9:00-9:30
  - 10:30-11:30
  - 12:00-12:30
  - 14:00-15:30
  - 16:30-17:00

Now, we need to find gaps where all four are free. We can break the day into 30-minute slots and check each one.

Let's list potential start times from 9:00 to 16:30 (since the meeting ends at 17:00 at the latest).

We'll check each possible 30-minute slot:

- 9:00-9:30: Alexander is busy (9:00-9:30) ❌
- 9:30-10:00: Dennis (9:30-10:00), Nancy (9:30-11:00), Alexander (10:30-11:30 but 9:30-10:00 is free? Actually Alexander is free at 9:30-10:00? But Dennis and Nancy are busy. ❌
- 10:00-10:30: 
  - Andrew: free
  - Dennis: free (next busy at 12:00)
  - Nancy: busy until 11:00 ❌ (so 10:00-10:30 is within 9:30-11:00)
  - Alexander: free until 10:30 (busy at 10:30-11:30) but 10:00-10:30 is free.
  But Nancy is busy until 11:00, so this slot is not free for Nancy. ❌

- 10:30-11:00:
  - Andrew: free
  - Dennis: free
  - Nancy: busy (9:30-11:00) ❌
  - Alexander: busy (10:30-11:30) ❌

- 11:00-11:30:
  - Andrew: free
  - Dennis: free
  - Nancy: free? (busy 9:30-11:00 and then 11:30-12:30, so 11:00-11:30 is free)
  - Alexander: busy (10:30-11:30) ❌

- 11:30-12:00:
  - Andrew: free
  - Dennis: free (next busy at 12:00)
  - Nancy: busy (11:30-12:30) ❌
  - Alexander: free (busy until 11:30, then free until 12:00) ✅ except Nancy

So 11:30-12:00: Nancy is busy (11:30-12:30) ❌

- 12:00-12:30:
  - All are busy: Dennis (12:00-12:30), Nancy (11:30-12:30), Alexander (12:00-12:30) ❌

- 12:30-13:00:
  - Andrew: free
  - Dennis: free (busy until 12:30)
  - Nancy: free (busy until 12:30)
  - Alexander: free (busy until 12:30) ✅

So 12:30-13:00 is free for all.

- 13:00-13:30: All should be free? Check:
  - Andrew: free
  - Dennis: free (no busy until 14:00)
  - Nancy: free (no busy until 14:00)
  - Alexander: free (no busy until 14:00) ✅

- 13:30-14:00: Similarly free ✅

- 14:00-14:30:
  - Dennis: busy (14:00-14:30) ❌
  - Nancy: busy (14:00-15:00) ❌

- 16:00-16:30:

  - Nancy: busy (16:00-16:30) ❌
  - Alexander: free (next busy at 16:30)

  - Alexander: busy (16:30-17:00) ❌

So the free slots where all are available are:

          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:

            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("You need to schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for half an hour on Monday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings.")

    """

```
